coredata.dc:title: Differences in the performance of broad-spectrum UV-absorbing carbon dots arising from diverse purification
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Weihao
Huang, Xinyu
Zhang, Yan
Liu, Yingliang
coredata.dc:description: Excessive ultraviolet radiation gradually affects human health, the growth of animals or plants and the stability of materials with the deterioration of global climate. In this paper, we used aromatic compounds to prepare the three-component water soluble carbon dots (Im-CDs) through the hydrothermal method, which has broad-spectrum ultraviolet (UV) absorption. However, the stability of CDs with UV absorption still presents certain challenges. It was found that the bottom-up synthesis of CDs generally results in the production of small molecular or oligo-fluorescent by-products. Inadequate purification can lead to misinterpretation of their properties and characteristics. Therefore, the prepared CDs were purified using conventional dialysis, prolonged dialysis, and silica gel column chromatography to compare the effects and errors of different purification methods on the results. Based on the resulting purification process, the structure model of the Im-CDs suggested that the carbon core possesses a core-shell structure along with weakly attached ligands on its surface rather than strong force. After chromatography, the Im-CDs had better heat, UV and acid-base resistance compared to other purification methods. The appropriate purification for CDs is therefore a crucial step in order to fulfill the prerequisites for practical applications. This is of great significance for the industrialization of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Broad-spectrum UV absorption
Purification
Structure model
Stability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The synthetic route of Im-CDs and purification process; (b) Schematic representation of the purification process for Im-CDs using various methodologies.

Ultraviolet absorption spectra of CDs of different reaction components (a) aromatic compounds; (b) aromatic compounds, EDA and CitNa; (c) organic acids/salts and EDA; (d) Organic acids/salts, EDA and 2-aminoimidazole sulphate.
(a) Raman spectra, (b)HRTEM, (c) AFM, (d)XPS, (e)FTIR and (f) UV-Vis of Im-CDs.
Schematic diagram of prolonged dialysis process.
HRTEM of (a) dialysate, (b) precipitate (black flocculent substances); (c) the photo of precipitate; (d) FTIR of precipitate.

UV absorption spectra of dialysate at different time, CDs and precipitate (a) 100–500 Da, (b) 1000 Da, (c) 2000 Da; FTIR of dialysate at different time, CDs and precipitate (d) 100–500 Da, (e) 1000 Da, (f) 2000 Da.
(a) Photo of Im-CDs chromatography process under UV light; (b) UV-Vis absorption spectra, (c) PL spectra and (d) FT-IR spectra of the separated components.
(a) Chromatographic separation of Im-CDs monitored by ESI-Q-TOF MS detection; (b) MALDI-TOF MS of Im-CDs.
Representative MS spectra of Im-CDs. (a-h) are the MS spectra of fractions 1–14 in different MS ranges.
XPS spectra of components 1(a, b, c, d), components 2 (e, f, g, h) and components 3 (i, j, k, l) of Im-CDs; high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (b, f, j), O1s (c, g, k) and N1s (d, h, l).
(a) 1H NMR and (b) 13C NMR of components 2 and components 3.
Schematic diagram of Im-CDs chromatographic separation and (a) UV-Vis, (b) H-NMR and (c) HRTEM.

Stability of Im-CDs treated with different purification methods. (a) conventional dialysis, (b) prolonged dialysis and (c) chromatography. (d) and (e) represent the attenuation curves for UVA and UVB.
Different carbon sources (organic acids or salts) and aromatic compounds.

head.ce:title: Differences in the performance of broad-spectrum UV-absorbing carbon dots arising from diverse purification

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive ultraviolet radiation gradually affects human health, the growth of animals or plants and the stability of materials with the deterioration of global climate. In this paper, we used aromatic compounds to prepare the three-component water soluble carbon dots (Im-CDs) through the hydrothermal method, which has broad-spectrum ultraviolet (UV) absorption. However, the stability of CDs with UV absorption still presents certain challenges. It was found that the bottom-up synthesis of CDs generally results in the production of small molecular or oligo-fluorescent by-products. Inadequate purification can lead to misinterpretation of their properties and characteristics. Therefore, the prepared CDs were purified using conventional dialysis, prolonged dialysis, and silica gel column chromatography to compare the effects and errors of different purification methods on the results. Based on the resulting purification process, the structure model of the Im-CDs suggested that the carbon core possesses a core-shell structure along with weakly attached ligands on its surface rather than strong force. After chromatography, the Im-CDs had better heat, UV and acid-base resistance compared to other purification methods. The appropriate purification for CDs is therefore a crucial step in order to fulfill the prerequisites for practical applications. This is of great significance for the industrialization of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The ultraviolet (UV) radiation from the sun that reaches the Earth's surface undergoes various complex absorption and scattering processes within the atmosphere. The presence of solar radiation is crucial for sustaining life on Earth. However, its ultraviolet component can also induce detrimental effects on both organic and inorganic entities. The spectrum of UV is typically divided into three distinct regions: UVA (315–400 nm), UVB (280–315 nm) and UVC (100–280 nm). [1] Especially, after the partial absorption of UVB and UVA, the ozone layer effectively blocks UVC radiation. The remaining ultraviolet portion consists of 98 % UVA and 2 % UVB. [2] UVA exhibits high penetration capabilities, facilitating the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in matrixes such as human skin and polymers, leading to long-term and chronic damage. [3] In contrast, UVB, which is only 2 %, has some regulatory effects on organisms, serving as an environmental signal and a potential abiotic stressor. The penetration power of this, however slightly weaker, is compensated by its high energy which results in immediate and severe damage to materials. For instance, elevated levels of UVB can induce damage to plants through DNA impairment, initiation of reactive oxygen species accumulation, and disruption of photosynthesis. [4] Exposure to UVB radiation may also hinder the immune response. [5] Polymers that absorb light at approximately 280 nm are susceptible to photooxidation of chromogenic or conjugated groups, resulting in the generation of free radicals and subsequent degradation. [6] Therefore, the application of UV absorbers needs to be considered urgently.

The UV absorber is a type of light stabilizer primarily utilized for the absorption of ultraviolet radiation emitted by sunlight and fluorescent light sources, thereby safeguarding human skin [7,8], plants [4], and polymer materials [9–11] against potential damage caused by ultraviolet rays. In order to protect the substrate from excessive ultraviolet damage, ultraviolet absorbents need to convert harmful ultraviolet rays into less harmful energy and show good stability, such as light, heat and acid-base stability. According to chemical structure and properties, ultraviolet absorbers can be classified into three categories: organic ultraviolet absorbers, inorganic ultraviolet absorbers, and natural ultraviolet absorbers. Organic UV absorbers primarily include benzophenones, benzotriazoles and triazines. These three classes of substances, which possess intramolecular hydrogen bonds, absorb UV radiation through a photoisomerization process of the hydroxyl group adjacent to the benzene ring and the benzene ring, and convert it into heat energy. [12] Moreover, inorganics such as TiO2, SiO2 and ZnO have been developed as inorganic UV absorbers and widely used in sunscreen cosmetics and polymer films, which benefit from the band gap absorption and scattering of light. [13] Apart from these, to adapt to high levels of ultraviolet radiation, plants trigger the production of antioxidant and UV-resistant compounds such as carotenoids (β-carotene and lycopene) [14], plant phenols (chlorogenic acid, tea polyphenols, grape seed extract) [15], and flavonoids (including flavanols, anthocyanins, and proanthocyanins) [16]. However, due to economic development, UV absorbers have become extensively utilized in industrial products and various daily consumables, including polymer films and sunscreen cosmetics. During transportation and usage, these compounds may undergo partial release into water and soil. Given that the majority of UV absorbers consist of organic compounds or inorganic particles that are resistant to decomposition, they tend to residue and accumulate within organisms, thereby exerting a discernible negative impact on both the ecosystem and its inhabitants. [17,18]

Carbon dots (CDs) or carbon-based materials are one of the hot topics in nano-materials research. They are zero-dimensional carbon-based compounds that exhibits excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and environmental friendliness. [19,20] In addition to showing excellent luminescence performance, recent studies have also reported on the ultraviolet absorption capabilities of CDs. Chunyan Liu et al. successfully prepared highly day-light photoluminescent and full band UV shielding SiGQDs nanofluid and their polymerized organic-inorganic hybrid Ormosil gel glasses via a facile and efficient technique. [21] Hongting Zhao et al. showed a B, N co-doped carbon dot that were synthesized and deposited on the surface of cotton fabric using the layer-by-layer self-assembly method, which exhibited excellent anti-UV properties. [22] Qiu et al. synthesized diverse carbon dots exhibiting excellent UV absorption and successfully employed them in the domains of film and sunscreen application, such as OP–CDs [23], N-CPDs [24], CDs@CaCO3 [25], His-CPDs [7].

In addition, in the rapid development of CDs, there are still different understandings of their composition, structure, luminescence source and purification process, especially the luminescence performance of CDs after purification. It is widely acknowledged that CDs have size effect, excitation wavelength dependence and fixed lattice spacing. [26–28] However, recent inquiries have arisen regarding the origin of luminescence in carbon nanodots, the degree of purification, and other related concerns. The fluorescence of CDs synthesized through bottom-up approaches originates from molecular fluorophores or their aggregation structures, which can exist as byproducts or even as the only products during the synthesis process. It has been demonstrated that the aggregation of molecular fluorophores in carbon dot solutions, particularly through hydrogen bonding interactions, can effectively mimic both the structural and optical properties exhibited by carbon dots. [29,30] The purification of CDs is generally performed by dialysis or chromatography. However, dialysis alone may not effectively separate carbon dots from raw materials, particularly in the case of bottom-up synthesized carbon dots. [31,32] It has been reported that certain bottom-up synthesized carbon dots contain oligomers or polymers with excellent fluorescence performance and no excitation dependence as their fluorescence source, which can be found in the dialysate during the process of dialysis. [33,34] Therefore, ensuring the purity of carbon samples and employing appropriate purification techniques can enable a more precise assessment of their performance.

To date, research on the UV absorption of CDs has been limited to experimental stages. However, in practical outdoor applications, carbon dots may exhibit instability under prolonged exposure to UV radiation, high temperatures, and high humidity. These instabilities include faster degradation of UVA compared to UVB and separation of CDs from the matrix. Such behaviors might be attributed to the purification method employed during the preparation process of carbon dots. Therefore, this study aims to enhance the stability of carbon dots in real-world applications by analyzing their raw material selection, purification methods, and structural characteristics. In this study, three-component water-soluble carbon dots (Im-CDs) were synthesized via hydrothermal method using sodium citrate, ethylenediamine and 2-aminoimidazole bisulfate as raw materials, which have excellent UVA and UVB full-band absorption performance. The effects of different organic acids/salts and aromatic compounds, pH, concentration on the UVA and UVB absorption properties and stability were investigated during the preparation process. It was found that Im-CDs prepared with nitrogen-containing aromatic heterocyclic compounds as raw materials showed stable UVB absorption, indicating resistance to UV radiation, high temperature and strong alkali condition. In addition, the prepared Im-CDs were purified by conventional dialysis, prolonged dialysis, and chromatography, and the differences in the real performance of the CDs evaluated by different purification methods were analyzed. The prolonged dialysis with different molecular weights were employed, and the dialysate and retention were characterized by fluorescence, ultraviolet absorption, IR, XPS, etc. After 4 days of dialysis, the dialysate contained strong UV absorption components while the retention agglomerated and did not emit light. The strong UV absorption of Im-CDs may be attributed to their molecular state. By means of chromatographic separation, the raw materials and by-products in the Im-CDs were effectively separated, thus obtaining IPCA and aminomethazole condensate with broad-spectrum UV absorption properties. Considering the observed dialysis phenomenon and the reported structural models, the structural analysis of Im-CDs reveals a core-shell structure for its carbon core, suggesting that weakly interacting ligands are attached to its surface rather than strongly interacting ones. Furthermore, after being purified by chromatography, Im-CDs exhibit better thermal stability, UV and alkaline resistance compared to the other two purification methods. Since there are no excess carboxyl or amino groups in the sample, no free radicals are produced. Therefore, adopting the correct purification method is crucial for a comprehensive evaluation of the various properties of CDs, which is of great significance for the research of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening and absorption comparison of raw materials
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of broad-spectrum UV-absorbing CDs was carried out by hydrothermal method using sodium citrate, ethylenediamine and aminoimidazole as raw materials in Fig. 1 . The citric acid matrix has been utilized as a precursor for the synthesis of high quantum yield (QY) luminescent moieties. Initially, citric acid and amine compounds were employed to synthesize citrazinic acid [35], while subsequent studies adopted a bottom-up approach to continue the carbonization of CDs with excellent fluorescence. [36,37] The UV absorption of CDs in the citric acid matrix has been systematically investigated in recent years. [38] However, the system is deficient in UVB absorption. According to Woodward-Fieser rule, aromatic compounds with conjugated structure can have strong UV absorption in the UVB range. On this basis, aromatic compounds with amino groups were selected to form a three-component system for hydrothermal reaction. After screening, the third component in Table 1 comprises a selection of UVB-absorbing aromatic compounds, including aniline derivatives and nitrogen-containing five-membered hybrid cyclic compounds. This is because some aromatic compounds as raw materials are not easy to carbonize and form carbon points in hydrothermal or solvent-thermal reaction. [39] Figs. 1(a) and S1(a) reveal that most single-component CDs lack UV absorption capacity during hydrothermal reaction, except for aminoimidazole (Im) and 4-amino-2-chlorophenol. When the aromatic compounds simultaneously react with sodium citrate and EDA, the resulting CDs incorporating amino imidazole sulfate or 4-amino-2-chlorophenol as the third component exhibit pronounced spectral UV absorption characteristics, particularly UVA and UVB (Figs. 1(b) and S1(b)). Compared with the two, the CDs with amino imidazole structure exhibited a more pronounced absorption peak at 293 nm. The absorption peak 3-aminopyrazole with a similar molecular structure was observed at 224 nm, which deviated from the expected UVB absorption pattern in Fig. 2(a). [40] Additionally, the CDs with hexahydroxy N-containing heterocyclic compound exhibit only one absorption peak in close proximity to UVA, which were not suitable for the raw materials of broad-spectrum ultraviolet absorption CDs (Table S1 and Fig. S2). According to the Woodward-Fieser rules, the larger the conjugated system, the more red-shifted the maximum absorption wavelength. Therefore, CDs made from aminopyrazine, 3-aminopyridazine, or 2-aminopyrimidine as raw materials exhibited a UV absorption that overlaps with UVA (Fig. S2(a)), compared to Im-CDs made from five-membered ring nitrogen-containing imidazole as raw materials. Relevant to the findings of aromatic compounds, an investigation was also conducted on organic acids/salts in Table 1. When three-component CDs were formed by the reaction of organic acid or salt with EDA and aminooimidazole, it can be observed from Fig. 2(c) and (d) that they exhibit distinct UV absorptions. All organic acids or salts can react with EDA to form CDs and have UVA absorption at about 320 nm, which provide the basis for the three-component system. The absorption peak of salts showed a red shift compared with that of organic acids, which may be related to pH value. Furthermore, the organic salts were more favorable for broad-spectrum absorption of UVA and UVB when reacted with aminooimidazole, thereby providing further evidence for the significant influence of pH value on the synthesis of three-component CDs. Therefore, the influence of pH in the synthesis process was also explored in Fig. S3. Under alkaline conditions, the absorption of UVB generated by aminooimidazole was significantly enhanced, with a direct correlation between pH value and UVB absorption strength. This phenomenon can be attributed to the deprotonation of the imidazole ring, which significantly influences its absorbance. [41–43] Based on the above results, the UV absorption of Im-CDs may be derived from the products formed by the reaction of IPCA(citrazinic acid derivatives) and amino imidazole. [37,44,45] Consequently, this finding established a solid foundation for potential applications in coatings.

Based on the aforementioned raw material screening, the three-component system comprising CitNa, EDA, and amino imidazole sulfate exhibited superior broad-spectrum ultraviolet absorption performance. Three distinct purification processes were conducted and characterized using techniques such as IR spectroscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV-Vis spectrophotometry, atomic force microscopy (AFM), among others.

The first purification method was the one used in most of the CDs synthesis literature [46–48]. Macromolecular particles were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter to obtain a yellow solution, followed by dialysis with deionized water for 24 h using a 2000 Da dialysis membrane to remove residual small molecules. The Im-CDs structure obtained by this purification is characterized followed as: the XRD diffraction of Im-CDs has a broad peak near 20° in Fig. 3(a), which belongs to amorphous carbon atoms. [49] Additionally, raman spectroscopy revealed distinct D and G peaks, which deviate from the reported CDs of the two-components in citric acid. [50] This discrepancy may be attributed to an enhanced level of graphitization in the carbon species following the introduction of Im. The size of Im-CDs was 2.9 nm, with lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, corresponding to (100) faces of graphite, as seen from HRTEM and AFM (Fig. 3(b) and (c)). the surface functional groups of the CDs were characterized using infrared absorption spectra. The surface of the Im-CDs was further analyzed using XPS in Figs. 3(d) and S4. The composition of Im-CDs consists of C, N and O with respective proportions of 60.65 %, 20.87 %, and 17.81 %. The high-resolution C1s spectra exhibited peaks at 284.95 eV, 286.02 eV, and 287.7 eV, corresponding to C-C/C=C, CO, CN, CO bonds. The peaks observed at 399.41 eV and 397.92 eV correspond to pyrrole-N(C-NH-C) and pyridine-N(N-C=N), respectively, indicating the successful binding of the imidazole molecule to the carbon surface [51]. Based on the relative intensities of these two peaks, it can be inferred that the imidazole molecules were predominantly distributed on the carbon surface. As depicted in Fig. 3(e), compared to the two-component Cit-Na CDs without aminoimidazole sulfate, the three-component Im-CDs exhibited additional characteristic absorptions at 1281 and 1158 cm−1 corresponding to imidazolium ring vibrations [52], in addition to the original OH, NH, CO, and CN bonds. Furthermore, owing to the formation of amide bonds through reaction between amino imidazolium molecules and carboxyl groups on the carbon dot surface, a distinct NH bending vibration associated with amide bonds was observed at 625 cm−1. For UV-Vis absorption spectra in Fig. 3(f), the 220 nm absorption in the UV-visible spectra arise from the π-π* conjugation of the carbon nucleus, while the 295 nm UVB absorption originated from the n-π* conjugation involving lone pair electrons on nitrogen in imidazole and the aromatic ring [53]. Additionally, the 347 nm UVA absorption and a minor shoulder at 400 nm were attributed to n-π* conjugation involving -COOH and -NH2.

To investigate the efficacy of dialysis in purifying CDs, the conventional dialysis purification process was further optimized by prolonging the duration of dialysis, employing dialysis membranes with varying molecular weights, and increasing the frequency of dialysate replacement.

Fig. 4 shows that dialysis membranes with different molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 100–500 Da, 1000 Da and 2000 Da were used for comparison to prolong the dialysis process. The dialysis procedure was in the supporting information. In the continuous replacement process of dialyzate, the unreacted raw materials, intermediate molecules, oligomers, and other small molecules present in the CDs solution (within the dialysis membrane) were effectively eliminated. Subsequently, after approximately 24 h, precipitate(black flocculent substances) appeared in the dialysis membrane (process ② in Fig. 4). The phenomenon of dialysis is shown in Fig. S5.

Most of the literature reports that CDs are connected by conjugation between carbon nucleus and surface ligands, or quasicrystalline carbon structure consisting of microscopic carbon clusters and a polymer framework. The interaction between surface ligands and CDs has a certain impact on their fluorescence, whether it is carbon core or molecular luminescence. [32,54–56] However, following prolonged dialysis and increased frequency of dialysate replacement, the majority of UV absorption components were observed to exit the dialysis membrane while the carbon nucleus exhibited agglomeration. It is assumed that the luminescent molecules as surface ligands attach to the surface of CDs with weak force. Under the condition of insufficient purification of CDs, the carbon nucleus can be fully dispersed in water due to the protection of ligands. However, during thorough dialysis, strong osmotic pressure inside and outside the dialysis membrane leaded to separation of fluorescent molecules from the carbon core into the dialyzate. Consequently, the carbon core lose their protection from ligands and undergo significant agglomeration. To verify that hypothesis, the dialysate of different time, the purified CDs solution and the black flocculent substances were characterized by HRTEM, UV-Vis, FTIR, PL and XPS.

Fig. 5 is HRTEM of the dialysate after freeze-drying and precipitate. The spherical particles in Fig. 5(a) had a diameter of approximately 50–60 nm, which was widely reported as carbonized polymer dots. [54,57] However, the spherical particles in Fig. 5(a) came from the dialysate, which indicated that the substance passed through the dialysis membrane. However, according to the test data table provided by Spectrum Labs, the pore diameters of the 100–500 Da dialysis membrane is merely 1 nm, rendering it impossible for such substantial particles to permeate through the dialysis membrane. The spheres might be dry nanocrystals formed by molecules that have undergone dialysis through hydrogen-bonded. [29] The precipitates within the dialysis membrane were examined using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). As depicted in Fig. 5(b) and (c), the black agglomeration was composed of numerous small particles measuring 3–4 nm in size, exhibiting a lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm. FTIR showed that the substance has peaks at 3485 and 1630 cm−1, respectively, which were -OH and CO, similar to the results of CQDs prepared by electrolytic graphite rod reported in literature. [58–60]

Moreover, the UV absorption and FTIR spectra in Fig. 6 , demonstrate that dialysates obtained at different time were identical to that of CDs obtained through conventional dialysis, as previously mentioned. In addition, the same results were obtained by using three different dialysis membranes for dialysis. The only difference was that the substances were dialyzed out of the 2000 Da dialysis membrane at the beginning, and the substances of the dialysis membrane with small molecular weight had a longer dialysis time, but negligible molecules were expelled after 96 h, according to various characteristics. The same results were observed for XPS in the dialysis process (Figs. S6 and S7) and PL (Fig. S8), which exhibit similarities to the dilution process of carbon-based solutions. Specifically, the PL results demonstrated that the maximum emission peaks of both dialysate and dialyzed CDs solution remained unchanged, indicating non-excitation dependence, a characteristic feature of molecular fluorescence. In addition to conducting a long-term dialysis experiment to verify the hypothesis, an extended investigation was performed to confirm the interaction between luminescent molecules and carbon core in Fig. S9. Based on the above results, the CDs solution after dialysis exhibit negligible emission and lack ultraviolet and infrared absorption, suggesting that the post-dialysis CDs solution was essentially devoid of any substances except for the aggregation of carbon core. Besides the imidazole tri-component CDs, similar phenomena are also observed in the other three-component CDs mentioned in Table 1, as well as in commonly encountered CA-CDs (citric acid + EDA) during prolonged dialysis.

However, dialysis fails to facilitate the separation of molecular substances or CDs, so that the major source of absorption cannot be known. Positive phase column chromatography was used to separate the various substances of Im-CDs in Fig. 7(a), with ethanol as the mobile phase. The observation reveals that, when exposed to UV light, the silicon gel column exhibited distinct blue fluorescence emissions at three different wavelengths due to its different polarity. The collected solutions were analyzed, and subsequently categorized into three main components or CDs. As depicted in Fig. 7(b), the Im-CDs solution comprises three components, namely component 1 with UVB absorption, component 3 with UVA absorption, and component 2 with broad-spectrum UV absorption (UVA and UVB). Among them, component 1 exclusively absorbed UVB about 307 nm. By comparing FT-IR and TG with the raw material (amino imidazole) depicted in Figs. S10 and S11, this particular component can be identified as the CDs predominantly composed of amino imidazole due to its low polarity, and its emission peak was 461 nm (Fig. 7(b)), which is different from the luminescence spectrum of the derivatives of IPCA. Furthermore, based on the dialysis image, it can be observed that the flow rate of component 3 was comparatively slower. The excitation and emission wavelengths of this component were measured to be 356 nm and 442 nm respectively, which was consistent with the characteristic spectra exhibited by Cit-Na CDs synthesized using sodium citrate and EDA. To provide additional evidence supporting this claim, chromatography was performed on Cit-Na CDs in Fig. S12. Only one separated component displayed absorption, excitation, emission wavelengths and IR similar to those of component 3. Hence, component 3 might be the CDs of the reaction between sodium citrate and EDA. Moreover, considering the low solubility of sodium citrate in ethanol, its relatively slow flow rate further supports the notion that component 3 represents a major product derived from IPCA. The separated components 1 and 3 can thus be proved to be individual or paired carbonization processes of the reactants, and thus exhibit monotonic UVA or UVB absorption. However, component 2 exhibited a wide range of UV absorption (Fig. 7(b)) and possessed polarities intermediate between those of component 1 and component 3, thereby supposing that this particular component corresponds to the CDs resulting from the reaction between amino imidazole and IPCA. In addition to the characteristic peak of IPCA, the FT-IR in Fig. 7(d) of this component also exhibited distinct vibrations at 1274 and 1138 cm−1 belonging to imidazole ring.

To further verify the above chromatographic results, MALDI-TOF MS and LC-MS were used to analyze Im-CDs, which has been an indispensable tool to determine the mass of CDs. Each section of Im-CDs labeled in Fig. 8 was subjected to high-resolution MS analysis, and the corresponding high-resolution MS of the eight sections was shown in Fig. 9 . The components of Im-CDs were fully separated by liquid chromatography with a mixture of methanol and water (M:W) as the mobile phase, M:W from 9:1 to 1:9. The samples with low polarity should be prioritized for separation. The high-resolution MS revealed that the initial components to separate were fragments with higher m/z values. In Fig. 9(a), the presence of condensation products formed by citric acid and amino imidazole can be observed, exhibiting m/z values of 322(M1) and 387(M2). Subsequently, the condensate of IPCA and aminoimidazole(M4), the condensate of citric acid and aminoimidazole(M7) and IPCA(M3) were separated, with m/z of 245, 257 and 180, respectively, and the corresponding fragments were detected in Fig. 9(b-c), d and (e-f). Finally, the unreacted citrate ions(m/z = 191) were separated from the remaining components (Fig. 9(g-h)). LC-MS analysis was also performed on the Cit-Na CDs in Fig. S13 and S14. The components separated from the CDs were IPCA (M1’, m/z = 180) and citric acid (M8’, m/z = 191), with IPCA being the lower polar fraction. In addition, it can be seen from the MALDI-TOF MS in Fig. 8(b) that the molecular weights of fluorescent molecules of Im-CDs are almost all less than 500(m/z), mainly concentrated in m/z = 250. The obtained results demonstrated the presence of M1 at m/z = 322, M2 at m/z = 387, M3 at m/z = 180, M4 at m/z = 245, M7 at m/z = 257 and citric acid (M11) at m/z = 191, which exhibited a high level of agreement with the LC-MS of Im-CDs. It demonstrated that most of the molecules with UV absorption of Im-CDs prepared by hydrothermal method were molecules with low degree of polymerization. This observation serves as a material foundation, enabling these molecules with UV absorption to effectively permeate through the dialysis membrane during long-term dialysis procedures as mentioned above. This was different from the results of the microwave heating method reported in the preparation of CNP of citric acid and ethylenediamine, as it leads to the formation of high molecular weight polymers. [61] In contrast, the hydrothermal method was relatively mild, resulting in a lower degree of polymerization of the product.

The results of LC-MS revealed that Im-CDs were divided into three main components with UV absorption, namely M2, M3 and M4. According to the polarity, the sequence of chromatographic separation using ethanol as mobile phase should be M2, M4, M3. XPS was used to further analyze components 1,2 and 3 in Fig. 10 . From the Fig. 10(b), (c) and (d), component 1 contains amide bond and pyrrole nitrogen structure but without hydroxy group, which can be inferred that it should be M2 structure. The XPS analysis of component 2 reveals the presence of CO of amide group (Fig. 10(f)) and pyridine and pyrrole nitrogen(Fig. 10(h)), indicating the inclusion of imidazole and IPCA. Besides, the component lacked carboxyl or hydroxyl groups and exhibited broad spectrum UV absorption in Fig. 7(b), indicating its formation as a result of the reaction between amino imidazole and IPCA. For component 3, XPS shows that it contains carbonyl, hydroxyl, and CO bond, but only pyridine nitrogen and no amide bond. Additionally, the slow elution suggested a high polarity of this component, leading to speculation that it may correspond to IPCA.

Given the facile analysis of component 1 through FTIR and TG, while components 2 and 3 exhibited intricate and similar structures, NMR was employed for further characterization. The 1H NMR spectrum of component 3 in Fig. 11(a) revealed signals at 2.5 ppm and 3.2 ppm, which can be attributed to the methylene group adjacent to the nitrogen atom. Additionally, resonances at 5.86 ppm and 7.96 ppm, 8.33 ppm correspond to the protons on IPCA moiety and amide bond respectively. [62] The hydrogen atoms of carboxyl groups often undergo proton exchange, especially in higher solvent polarity or in the presence of higher concentrations of water molecules in the solvent. Therefore, there may not be a clear carboxyl hydrogen signal visible in the NMR. Among them, the sample contained a trace amount of H2O-H at 4.7 ppm. In 13C NMR (Fig. 11(b)), the chemical shifts of 42.78 and 40.79 ppm correspond to methylene groups connected with nitrogen, while the range of 150–160 ppm represents multiple peaks associated with aromatic ring carbons. Additionally, the chemical shifts between 174 and 182 ppm indicate carboxyl or carbonyl carbon resonances. The peaks observed at 1.81 and 1.02 ppm correspond to the ethanol. For component 2, in addition to the previously mentioned chemical shifts of component 3, the chemical shifts of 4-H, 5-H, and 2-H belonging to the imidazole ring were observed at 6.37 ppm and in the range of 7.0–8.0 ppm. Additionally, the 13C NMR exhibited peaks at 121.91 ppm and 126.38 ppm corresponding to the 4-C and 5-C of imidazole ring, respectively, while a peak at 137.46 ppm indicated 2-C linked with N atom. Thus, component 2 was the CDs with broad-spectrum UV absorption obtained by reacting with the third component-amino imidazole on the basis of component 3. However, the lack of precise control over the hydrothermal reaction resulted in a diverse range of components present in unpurified CDs, necessitating stringent purification methods for their refinement. Moreover, according to the literature [63,64], when molecules are attached to the surface of CDs, their 1H NMR peaks exhibit broadening instead of sharpness. As depicted in the illustration in Fig. 11(a), component 2 and component 3 displayed broad peaks around the aromatic ring. Hence, it can be inferred that these molecules are likely bound to the surface of carbon core.

Based on the above three separation results, it can be concluded that after prolonged dialysis of Im-CDs, most UV absorption components permeated through the dialysis membrane while carbon core in the membrane underwent agglomeration without emission. HRTEM images presented in Fig. S15 demonstrated that ligands were separated from carbon core during the middle stage of dialysis. Ultimately, upon completion of dialysis, the ligands and carbon core were completely separated, with the ligands outside the dialysis membrane and the carbon core inside the dialysis membrane.

During the chromatographic process, Im-CDs can be fractionated into three components, with component 2 exhibiting broad-spectrum UV absorption. The identification of this component as a reaction product between IPCA and amino imidazole was confirmed by LC-MS, XPS, and NMR analyses. It is worth noting that after the removal of raw materials and by-product from CDs, HRTEM in Fig. 12 showed that it had a structure similar to the core-shell with numerous ligands coating the carbon core surface. Considering the dialysis phenomenon observed and in conjunction with structural models proposed, the structure model of the Im-CDs suggested that the carbon core possesses a core-shell structure along with weakly attached ligands on its surface rather than strong force. Because during the prolonged dialysis process, only by repeatedly changing the permeable liquid, i.e. increasing the osmotic pressure, can the ligand and the carbon core be separated. Only osmotic pressure caused gradual detachment of these ligands from the carbon core resulting in agglomeration (Figs. 5 and S15).

By employing the aforementioned three methods for the purification of Im-CDs, in addition to the differences in characterization of each purification method, the stability of the purified Im-CDs was also compared. The UV absorption aging changes of Im-CDs after conventional dialysis, prolonged dialysis, and chromatographic treatment were illustrated in Fig. 13(a), (b), and (c), respectively. According to the Formula (1) of the absorbance decay law:
(1)
A
t
=
A o
e
− kt
where A(t) is the absorbance at time t, A0 is the initial absorbance, and k is the decay constant, which represents the proportion of absorbance that decreases per unit of time. By performing a fit on the logarithmic values, the decay constant k can be obtained, thus determining the decay rate.

The UVA decay rates (Fig. 13(d)) of Im-CDs after conventional dialysis, prolonged dialysis, and chromatography were
8.5 ×
10
− 2
, 2.0 ×
10
− 2
, and 6.8 ×
10
− 11
, respectively, while the corresponding UVB decay rates (Fig. 13(e)) were 6.5 ×
10
− 2
, 3.6 ×
10
− 2
, and 3.4 ×
10
− 3
. It is evident that the attenuation rate of UVA is greater than that of UVB, which can be attributed to the fact that carboxyl and amino groups are primarily responsible for absorbing UVA while imidazole ring accounts for UVB absorption. Under conditions of high temperature, humidity, and strong UV radiation, carboxyl and amino groups were prone to oxidation resulting in a more rapid attenuation of UVA. Comparing the three purification methods, the Im-CDs after chromatographic separation showed the best stability, with the smallest decay rates for UVA and UVB. This indicated that the raw materials and by-products in the CDs can affect their stability, such as excessive free amino and carboxyl groups. Particularly, the amino group is susceptible to electron transfer reactions upon exposure to strong UV radiation, resulting in the generation of free radical cations and anions that induce CDs damage, ultimately compromising their stability. Additionally, the presence of excess raw materials and by-products in Im-CDs can lead to the generation of free radicals when applied to UV-absorbing products such as plastics and rubbers. These free radicals could initiate further chemical reactions, including molecular chain breakage, thereby impacting the mechanical properties and durability of the material. Consequently, although carboxyl and amino groups exhibited different responses to UV radiation, they may both contribute to the formation of free radicals in specific situations, thus influencing the stability of CDs and their application products.

Furthermore, the pH stability of UVA and UVB of Im-CDs was investigated following treatment with three different purification methods. For the stability of UVA (Fig. S16 and Table S2) and UVB (Fig. S17 and Table S3) under different pH conditions, the stability of the sample purified by conventional dialysis seems to be higher than that of the samples purified by the other two methods in acidic conditions. However, this was not the true stability of Im-CDs, as the samples treated with conventional dialysis still have a large number of raw materials remaining, including sodium citrate and EDA, which are both basic, thus raising the pH of the solution. This was undoubtedly misleading the true results by the raw materials. For prolonged dialysis, its stability was similar to that of conventional dialysis, but the rate of decay was slightly faster, which may be due to the fact that it reduced the raw materials and by-products, but does not completely eliminate the effects. Following chromatography, the sample devoid of any residual raw materials and by-products exhibited stability under alkaline conditions for 240 h, demonstrating a lower decay rate for both UVA and UVB radiation. The aforementioned observation further suggested that Im-CDs exhibited remarkable stability in maintaining their broad-spectrum UV absorption properties in alkaline environments (Fig. S16(e) and (f)). This finding established a solid material foundation for the potential application of Im-CDs as UV absorbers in coatings or rubber industries, considering that coatings, rubber, and other high molecular weight substances typically contain alkaline components. Consequently, this discovery provided a reliable basis for future research on coatings. The stability obtained from this also further indicates that the purification of CDs is of extremely important significance for the evaluation of their performance results.

In the paper, Im-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using sodium citrate, ethylenediamine, and aminoguanidine sulfate as raw materials, which exhibited excellent broad-spectrum UV absorption properties (including UVA and UVB). To evaluate the performance and stability of Im-CDs after purification, three methods—traditional dialysis, extended dialysis, and chromatography—were employed. Traditional dialysis could not completely separate the raw materials and by-products in the CDs solution, which may affect the characterization results and hinder accurate evaluation of the CDs. Then, the Im-CDs were treated with prolonged dialysis and increased frequency of changing the dialysis solution. This method could only separate the molecular state substances and the carbon core, but the molecular state with UV absorption properties and the raw materials could not be completely separated, and no purification effect was achieved. Moreover, it was found that the molecular state with UV absorption properties acted as a surface ligand and weakly adhered to the surface of the CDs. This phenomenon only appeared after prolonged dialysis and frequent replacement of the dialysis solution to increase the osmotic pressure. Finally, when the chromatography method was used, the original carbon dots solution was separated into three components and it was found that only component 2 was the true broad-spectrum UV-absorbing carbon dots. Then, the carbon dots purified by chromatography had better thermal stability, UV resistance, and acid and alkali stability.

In conclusion, a reasonable purification method is crucial for comprehensive research on CDs, especially for small molecule CDs synthesized by the “bottom-up” method. This not only provides reliable judgments for subsequent applications, but also has significant implications for the industrialization of CDs.

Weihao Ye: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xinyu Huang: Data curation, Validation. Yan Zhang: Visualization, Investigation. Yingliang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.
